%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Section Objective:** Recognize and apply the key trigonometric identities listed in Appendix B.8-6, and know when each identity is useful in problem-solving.

Section B.8-6 collects the most important trigonometric identities you will encounter in signals and systems work. These include product-to-sum formulas, sum-to-product formulas, power-reduction identities, and the combined sinusoid formula. Rather than deriving them from scratch each time, you should recognize their structure on sight and know which one to reach for when simplifying an expression or solving an exam problem. After this section, you will be able to identify the correct identity for a given expression, apply it confidently, and avoid the most common sign and coefficient traps.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The four identity groups in B.8-6. The combined sinusoid formula (bottom right) is the highest-yield item for exams.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777220133465-9301.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in B.8-6 is the **combined sinusoid formula**:

$$
a\cos x + b\sin x = C\cos(x + \theta), \quad C = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2},\quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-b}{a}\right)
$$

This tells you that any sum of a cosine and a sine at the **same frequency** collapses into a single cosine with a new amplitude \(C\) and a phase shift \(\theta\). The amplitude \(C\) is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by \(a\) and \(b\).

The supporting identities — product-to-sum, sum-of-angles, and power-reduction — are tools that often appear as intermediate steps leading toward this form.

**Minimal example:** \(3\cos x + 4\sin x\) gives \(C = \sqrt{9+16} = 5\) and \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(-4/3)\), so the result is \(5\cos(x + \theta)\).

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

The sign inside \(\tan^{-1}\) is \(-b/a\), **not** \(b/a\). Dropping the negative sign is the single most frequent error on this identity.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Product-to-sum identities convert a product of trig functions into a sum, simplifying integration and analysis.
- The combined sinusoid formula merges \(a\cos x + b\sin x\) into one cosine with amplitude \(\sqrt{a^2+b^2}\).
- The phase angle uses \(\tan^{-1}(-b/a)\) — the negative sign on \(b\) is a frequent exam trap to watch.

*In the next section, B.8-7, we turn to common derivative formulas, which build directly on the chain rule and product rule you will need for system analysis.*
